title: Authoring Page
text:
8 Placing Colorbars
Colorbars indicate the quantitative extent of image data. Placing in a figure is non-trivial because room needs to be made for them. The simplest case is just attaching a colorbar to each axes:1.
1 See the Matplotlib Gallery to explore colorbars further

Code
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

fig, axs = plt.subplots(2, 2)
fig.set_size_inches(20, 8)
cmaps = ['RdBu_r', 'viridis']
for col in range(2):
    for row in range(2):
        ax = axs[row, col]
        pcm = ax.pcolormesh(
          np.random.random((20, 20)) * (col + 1),
          cmap=cmaps[col]
        )
        fig.colorbar(pcm, ax=ax)
plt.show()

title: Chip Brock
text: I’m an experimental particle physicist at Michigan State University and have been privileged for 50 years to be supported by the U.S. public to do particle physics research. I’m grateful for that support and my way of paying you back is by teaching these subjects to non-science students and to now broaden my audience to you.

title: About QS&BB
text:
I’m an experimental particle physicist who’s devoted the last 20 years to teaching two home-grown courses to non-science university students at Michigan State University. The first is called Quarks, Spacetime, and the Big Bang—which sort of tells you the content, The second is Navigating the Universe, which is a history of physics and art for MSU honors students.
The blog to this site—Quarks, Spacetime, and the Big Bang (QS&BB)— is a different way of teaching. In bite-sized chunks I bring some of the topics from these courses to the public and teach some of the topics of my 50 years of research at the Fermi National Accelerator Laboratory in Illinois and CERN’s Large Hadron Collider in Geneva, Switzerland.
